root: ^Satan descends from Mount Niphates into the green world below. Every step brings new sensations — the softness of moss beneath feet accustomed to burning stone, the scent of flowers that have no name yet, the sound of water running over rocks with no purpose but to be beautiful.

^He has seen Heaven. He has ruled in Hell. But Eden is something else entirely — a world made not for angels or demons but for creatures of earth, and it wears its beauty without grandeur, without ceremony, without any knowledge that it is beautiful at all.
^Satan finds a perch among the branches of a great tree — the Tree of Life, tallest in the garden — and looks down upon Paradise.

^Below him, the garden unfolds. Rivers divide and rejoin, threading through groves of fruit trees. Animals move without fear — the lion lies beside the lamb, the serpent glides through grass without malice. Birds sing in harmonies that would shame the choirs of Heaven.
^And there, at the centre of it all, are the two.

^They walk together through the garden, hand in hand. They are tall and beautiful — more beautiful than Satan expected. Their bodies are unclothed and unashamed, moving with the easy grace of creatures who have never known pain or self-consciousness.
^Adam speaks to Eve, pointing at the sky, naming the clouds. Eve laughs — and the sound carries through the garden like the first music ever made.
^Satan watches. And something inside him cracks.
^(What does Satan feel as he watches them?)
^(He remembers what he was — before the war, before the fall.)
^(He feels nothing. He has a mission. Sentiment is a luxury he cannot afford.)

c-0: ^Not envy of their beauty — Satan has beauty enough, even ruined. Not envy of their garden — he has built Pandemonium, and it is more magnificent. But envy of their innocence. Their ease. The simple, effortless way they belong to each other and to this place.
^They do not know what it is to be divided from the thing you love most. They do not know what it is to look at the sky and see only the ceiling of your prison.
^They do not know, and Satan hates them for it.
c-1: ^There was a time — how long ago? Eternity has no calendar — when Satan walked in light as they do now. When he too moved through beauty without questioning it, without grasping at it, without the constant gnawing fear that it would be taken away.
^He was Lucifer then. The Morning Star. Brightest of all God's angels. And he was happy — or something so close to happiness that the difference did not matter.
^That being is dead now. But watching Adam and Eve, Satan feels the ghost of him stir.
c-2: ^Satan forces his gaze to become analytical. The garden's defences — none visible. The creatures' awareness of danger — nonexistent. The tree at the centre — the one God has forbidden — unguarded, its fruit hanging heavy and ripe.
^This will be easy. Almost insultingly easy.

^And yet... his eyes keep drifting back to their faces. To the way Eve tilts her head when she listens. To the way Adam's hand rests on her shoulder as if it belongs there, as if it has always belonged there.
^Satan looks away. He has work to do.

the_soliloquy: ^The afternoon light shifts. Adam and Eve move deeper into the garden, gathering fruit, tending flowers — the light labour God has given them, not as punishment but as pleasure. Satan follows from above, leaping silently from branch to branch.
^And then, alone in the canopy of the Tree of Life, the dam breaks.
^O Sun, to tell thee how I hate thy beams, that bring to my remembrance from what state I fell, how glorious once above thy sphere.
^His voice is low, raw, stripped of the commanding tone he uses before his legions. This is Satan speaking to himself — or to the sun, or to God, or to no one at all.
^Till pride and worse ambition threw me down, warring in Heaven against Heaven's matchless King. Ah, wherefore? He deserved no such return from me, whom He created what I was...
^The words come slowly. Each one costs him something.
^Satan pauses. Below, a brook catches the sunlight and scatters it into a thousand golden fragments. He watches the light play on the water and continues.
^(Let Satan's remorse deepen. Perhaps he could still repent.)
^(Let his pride reassert itself. Remorse is weakness.)
^(Let him acknowledge the truth — he chose this, and he would choose it again.)

c-0: ^Is there no place left for repentance? No place left for pardon?

^The question hangs in the golden air. For a moment — just a moment — something flickers across Satan's face that might be hope. If he knelt now, if he prostrated himself before the sun and cried out...

^But even as the thought forms, he knows its shape. Repentance would mean submission. Submission would mean kneeling before the throne he swore to overthrow. And even if God forgave — as perhaps He might — what then? Return to servitude? Stand again in the ranks of the obedient, singing hymns of praise while the memory of freedom burns?
^None left but by submission, and that word disdain forbids me.
c-1: ^The moment of weakness passes like a cloud across the sun. Satan straightens on his branch. His jaw sets.
^What could be less in me than to claim power over this world? These wretched creatures are nothing. Dust given breath. And God sets them above the angels — above me?
^The old fire returns. The old certainty. He was right to rebel. He was right to refuse to kneel. If God's justice means placing mud-born infants in the garden while the firstborn burn...
^Then God's justice is tyranny by another name.
^So farewell hope, and with hope farewell fear, farewell remorse. All good to me is lost. Evil, be thou my good!
c-2: ^Satan's face hardens into something beyond anger — a cold, clear acceptance.
^Which way I fly is Hell; myself am Hell. And in the lowest deep a lower deep, still threatening to devour me, opens wide, to which the Hell I suffer seems a Heaven.

^He chose rebellion. He chose war against the Almighty. He lost, and he fell, and he burned. And given the chance to choose again — standing here in this garden of impossible beauty, watching these innocent creatures live the life he can never have...
^He would choose the same. Because the alternative is submission. And Satan would rather reign in Hell than serve in Heaven. That truth has not changed. It will never change.
^So farewell hope, and with hope farewell fear, farewell remorse. All good to me is lost. Evil, be thou my good!

the_resolve: ^The soliloquy ends. The sun continues its arc across Eden's sky, indifferent to the anguish of the creature in the treetop.
^Satan is still for a long time. Then he shakes himself — physically, like an animal shedding water — and turns his attention back to the garden below.
^Adam and Eve have settled near a bower of roses and jasmine. They sit close together, speaking softly. Satan descends to a lower branch, close enough to hear.
^Eve, tell me — what do you remember of your first day? When you first opened your eyes and saw... all of this?
^Eve is quiet for a moment, remembering.

^I woke beside a lake. I did not know what I was. I saw my reflection in the water and thought it was another creature — and I reached for it, and it reached for me. I might have stayed there forever, gazing at that image, if a voice had not called me away.
^His voice. God's. He told me that what I saw was only my own reflection — and that He would lead me to the one whose image I truly was. And He brought me to you.
^Satan listens. His claws dig into the bark.
^(Their love enrages him. It is everything he has lost.)
^(Their love interests him. It is a vulnerability he can exploit.)

c-0: ^They have each other. They have this garden. They have the love of their Creator. They have everything, everything, everything — and they did nothing to earn it.
^Satan's hands shake. The bark cracks beneath his grip.
^He was the firstborn of light. He sang the morning into being. He led the hosts of Heaven in their eternal dance. And for one act of defiance — one refusal to kneel — all of it was taken.
^And these... these things of clay are given Paradise as a gift.
^The injustice is a wound that will never heal. And wounded things bite.
c-1: ^Love. There it is — the weakness he was looking for.
^They are bound to each other. Each is the other's world. Which means that to corrupt one is to corrupt both. Eve loves Adam — she will follow him into ruin. Adam loves Eve — he will follow her.

^Satan's mind works with the cold precision of a general surveying a battlefield. Strike at Eve — she is newer to the world, more curious, more susceptible to wonder. If Eve falls, Adam falls. If Adam falls...
c-2: ^Satan watches them and feels... something. Not envy, not rage, but something older and quieter. A recognition.

^He loved once too. Not like this — not this gentle, domestic, human love — but with a fierce and burning devotion. He loved God. He loved Heaven. He loved the morning star he was named for and the music of the spheres.
^And then love curdled into pride, and pride into war, and war into this — a fallen angel hiding in a tree, watching two creatures live the life he destroyed for himself.
^For a moment, Satan almost pities himself. Then the moment passes, and what replaces it is worse than pity.

the_plan: ^Night falls on Eden. The first stars appear — different from Hell's starless void, different from the cold fixed stars above the cosmos. These stars are gentle, almost domestic, twinkling above the garden like candles in a nursery.
^Adam and Eve retire to their bower. Their evening prayer rises through the branches where Satan crouches.
^Thou also mad'st the night, Maker Omnipotent, and thou the day. This delicious place, for us too large, where thy abundance wants partakers...
^Satan flinches at the prayer. The sound of genuine worship — worship freely given, not compelled — is a blade that cuts deeper than any sword Michael wielded in the war.
^They sleep. The garden sleeps. Only Satan remains awake, turning plans over in his mind like a jeweller examining stones.
^The tree. The forbidden tree. God's single prohibition — of all the trees in the garden, you shall not eat of the Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil.
^Why forbid it? Unless the fruit has power. Unless knowledge is the crack in the armour of innocence.
^He will use the forbidden tree. The fruit itself will be the weapon.
^He needs more time to study them. Their habits, their weaknesses, their routines.
^He should approach Eve directly, in disguise, and plant the first seeds of doubt.

c-0: ^The tree stands at the centre of the garden, its fruit gleaming faintly in the starlight. Satan regards it from his perch.

^God gave them everything except this one fruit. One small denial in an ocean of abundance. And yet it is the denial that will matter — because what is forbidden becomes fascinating, and what is fascinating becomes desired, and what is desired...
^Satan smiles. It is not a pleasant smile.
^He knows what he will do. He knows how he will do it. He needs only the right moment and the right form.
c-1: ^Patience. The lesson of Hell, learned in fire and darkness.

^Satan settles into the tree like a bird of prey settling on a rooftop. He will watch. He will wait. He will learn the rhythms of this little world — when Adam goes alone to tend the eastern groves, when Eve wanders by herself among the flowers, when the angels who guard this place change their watches.
^Every fortress has a weakness. Every paradise has a gate that can be opened from within.
^He has time. And time, in Satan's hands, is a weapon.
c-2: ^Why wait? Eve is the key — Satan saw it in the way she spoke of her first day, the wonder in her voice, the curiosity that flickers behind her eyes like a flame behind glass.
^She wants to know. She wants to understand. And the Tree of Knowledge stands there, day after day, heavy with answers she has been forbidden to taste.
^Satan begins to plan his approach. Not as himself — the fallen archangel would terrify her. He needs a form that is cunning but not threatening, subtle but capable of speech.
^His eyes fall on a serpent coiled among the branches of a nearby tree, its scales catching the starlight.

the_end: ^The night deepens. Eden sleeps in perfect peace — the last perfect peace it will ever know, though it does not know this yet.
^In the Tree of Life, Satan keeps his vigil. Below him, Adam and Eve breathe softly in their bower, wrapped in each other's arms, dreaming whatever the unfallen dream.

^Above, the stars turn in their courses. The moon casts silver light across the garden. Somewhere beyond the walls of Eden, an angel patrol passes — Ithuriel and Zephon, sent by Gabriel to guard the sleeping pair.
^They do not think to look up. They do not imagine that evil has already found its way inside.
^Satan watches the angels pass. He does not move. He barely breathes.
^The serpent has entered the garden. And the garden does not yet know it.
